title: 1 Timothy 5
subtitle: Widows, Elders, and Church Discipline

heb.paragraph: The noun χήρα (chēra) appears nine times in this chapter alone, more than in any other NT chapter. In the Greco-Roman world, widows were among the most vulnerable members of society, lacking the legal and economic protections that marriage provided. The early church developed a formal system of widow care (as early as Acts 6:1) that evolved into an enrolled order with specific qualifications (vv. 9–10). Paul’s instructions here represent the most detailed social-welfare legislation in the NT.

refs.note: The earliest church conflict over widow care in Jerusalem—Hellenistic widows being overlooked in the daily distribution—shows that widow support was a foundational concern of the church from its inception.
James defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows in their distress, confirming that widow care was a cross-traditional priority in early Christianity.
OT legislation consistently protects widows, orphans, and aliens, establishing the covenantal basis for the church’s social responsibility.

notes.note: The church is a family. Treat older men as fathers, younger men as brothers, older women as mothers, younger women as sisters—with all purity. Pastoral relationships must reflect familial love and respect.
Genuine widows who have no family support deserve the church’s care. But if a widow has children or grandchildren, they must learn to care for their own family first—this is pleasing to God.

The truly desolate widow puts her hope in God and continues in prayer. But the one who lives for pleasure is dead even while she lives. If anyone does not provide for relatives, especially for household members, he has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.

Paul provides a template for pastoral relationships built on family metaphor: respect for age, purity with the young, and provision for the genuinely needy. The sharp rebuke that one who neglects family is worse than an unbeliever reveals how seriously Paul takes the obligation of domestic provision—it is not merely a social duty but a test of faith.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'truly widows' (τὰς ὄντως χήρας) distinguishes between widows who have no family support and those who do—only the former qualify for church assistance.
The statement that one who neglects family provision ‘has denied the faith’ is one of the strongest ethical judgments in the Pauline corpus, equating neglect of domestic duty with apostasy.

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The family metaphor (πατέρα/ἀδελφούς/μητέρας/ἀδελφάς) establishes the ecclesiological framework: the church is God’s household, and pastoral conduct must reflect familial norms. The qualifier ἐν πάσῃ ἁγνείᾳ (in all purity) especially governs Timothy’s interaction with younger women—an essential precaution in a context where false teachers were apparently targeting women (2 Tim 3:6).

The widow instruction distinguishes between ὄντως χήραι (genuine widows) and those with family resources. The church’s charitable obligation is triggered by genuine destitution, not mere marital status. The hyperbolic judgment of v. 8 (‘worse than an unbeliever’) reflects the ancient Mediterranean honor/shame code: even pagans care for their own.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The household code material transitions from theological instruction to practical social organization. The family metaphor in vv. 1–2 is not merely illustrative but constitutive: the church’s identity as οἶκος θεοῦ (3:15) determines how its members relate. The widow legislation (vv. 3–16) represents the most developed social-welfare policy in the NT, addressing both the rights of the vulnerable and the church’s limited economic capacity. The harsh judgment on neglectful family members (v. 8) reflects core Pauline ethics: faith must produce action.

hist.context: The instructions about treating church members as family (vv. 1–2) establish the pastoral tone for the more detailed regulations that follow. The extended widow passage (vv. 3–16) addresses both charitable support and an enrolled order of widows who served the community. In Greco-Roman Ephesus, a wealthy widow could exercise considerable social influence, while an impoverished one was utterly dependent on family or patronage networks. Paul’s concern is both pastoral (caring for the genuinely destitute) and administrative (preventing abuse of the church’s limited resources).

heb.gloss: to be enrolled, to be put on the list
heb.paragraph: The verb καταλέγω (katalegō) appears only here in the NT and specifically denotes formal enrollment on an official register. This is not casual membership but a recognized ecclesial order with binding commitments. The enrolled widow pledged herself to prayer and service, receiving financial support in return—an early form of what would later develop into women’s religious orders.

refs.note: Tabitha/Dorcas exemplifies the kind of widow ministry Paul describes: a woman devoted to good works and charity whose service was so valued that Peter was summoned to raise her from the dead.
Paul’s earlier teaching on the advantages of singleness for undistracted devotion to the Lord provides the theological backdrop for the enrolled widow order’s commitment.

notes.note: The enrolled widow must be at least sixty years old, faithful in marriage, and known for good deeds: raising children, showing hospitality, washing the saints’ feet, helping the afflicted. This is a woman of proven character.

Younger widows should not be enrolled because their desires may lead them away from their commitment to Christ. Some have already turned aside to follow Satan. Paul’s counsel: let them remarry, bear children, and manage their homes.
If any believing woman has widows in her family, she should assist them. The church’s resources must be reserved for those who are truly destitute.

The enrolled widow order combined charitable support with ministerial service. Paul sets strict criteria because the church’s resources are limited and its reputation is at stake. Younger widows should remarry rather than make pledges they cannot keep. The goal is not ascetic heroism but sustainable, faithful service.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase εἱνὸς ἀνδρὸς γυνή (‘one-man woman’) mirrors the overseer requirement of 3:2 (‘one-woman man’), establishing marital fidelity as a universal criterion for recognized ministry roles.

Paul’s instruction for younger widows to marry and bear children may seem to contradict 1 Cor 7:8–9, but the contexts differ: here the concern is preventing the practical problems caused by unfulfilled pledges, not establishing an absolute principle.

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The καταλεγέσθω (enrollment) criteria define a formal order: age (μὴ ἔλαττον ἐτῶν ἑξήκοντα, not less than sixty years), marital fidelity, and a catalog of good works. The foot-washing reference places this within a Johannine-Lukan tradition of humble service (cf. John 13; Luke 7:44).

The younger-widow instruction is pastorally pragmatic rather than theologically prescriptive. The πρῶτην πίστιν (first pledge/faith) they violate is their enrollment commitment, not saving faith. Paul’s preference that they remarry reverses the general Pauline preference for singleness (1 Cor 7) precisely because the Ephesian situation makes singleness a vehicle for idleness and gossip.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The enrolled widow institution reflects an intersection of Jewish charitable tradition and Greco-Roman patronage structures. The requirement of demonstrated service (foot-washing, hospitality, affliction-relief) shows that enrollment was earned through a proven track record, not merely claimed by virtue of marital status and age. The younger-widow caution is not misogynistic but situationally specific: in the Ephesian context, idle younger widows were evidently being recruited by the false teachers (cf. 2 Tim 3:6–7), making premature enrollment a pastoral liability.

hist.context: The enrolled widow order represents an early form of institutional church life distinct from the charismatic structures of Paul’s earlier letters. The age requirement (at least 60), marital fidelity criterion (‘wife of one husband’—the female counterpart of the overseer requirement), and good-works resume (washing feet, helping the afflicted) suggest a semi-formal ministerial role. Paul’s caution about younger widows (vv. 11–15) reflects both pastoral concern and the practical reality that the enrolled order required a permanent commitment that younger women might not sustain.

sections.header: Verses 17–25 — "Elders Who Direct Well Deserve Double Honor"

heb.paragraph: The phrase διπλῆς τιμῆς (double honor) is debated: does τιμή mean ‘honor’ in the abstract sense or ‘honorarium/payment’? The context (v. 18, quoting Deut 25:4 and citing the worker’s wage) strongly supports a financial component: elders who lead well deserve both respect and remuneration. The ‘double’ likely means ‘generous’ rather than literally twice a specific amount.

refs.note: Paul quotes this OT law (‘do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain’) as he does in 1 Cor 9:9, applying it to ministerial remuneration—a consistent hermeneutical move.
The two-or-three-witnesses rule for legal proceedings is applied to accusations against elders, establishing due process in church discipline.
Jesus’ teaching on church discipline provides the broader framework for the procedures Paul outlines here.

notes.note: Elders who rule well deserve double honor—both respect and financial support. The laborer deserves his wages. Those who work hard at preaching and teaching are especially worthy of generous support.

Do not receive an accusation against an elder except on the testimony of two or three witnesses. But those who persist in sin should be rebuked publicly, so that the rest may take warning. This charge is given before God, Christ Jesus, and the elect angels—impartiality is not optional.
Do not be hasty in the laying on of hands. Ordination is a serious matter. If you commission the wrong person, you share in his sins. Keep yourself pure.

Paul adds a personal note about Timothy’s health: stop drinking only water and use a little wine for your stomach. Some sins are obvious and precede their owners to judgment; others trail behind. Good deeds are equally evident.

Paul addresses the honor, discipline, and selection of elders with remarkable care. Those who govern well merit both respect and remuneration. Accusations must be properly attested. Public rebuke is required for persistent sin. Ordination must not be hasty, for the ordainer shares responsibility for those he commissions. The parenthetical about wine is a tender pastoral touch, reminding us that spiritual strictness does not require neglect of bodily health.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase διπλῆς τιμῆς likely refers to generous financial remuneration rather than abstract ‘double respect,’ given the supporting quotation about the laborer’s wage in v. 18.
The wine recommendation is a parenthetical health instruction, not a theological statement. It may address Timothy’s ascetic tendencies influenced by the false teachers’ abstinence demands (4:3).

mounce.source: William D. Mounce, Pastoral Epistles, WBC (2000) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The πρεσβύτεροι (elders) here are clearly office-holders, not simply older men. The διπλῆς τιμή (double honor) combines respect and remuneration, as confirmed by the Deut 25:4 citation and the dominical logion (‘the worker deserves his wages,’ cf. Luke 10:7). This is the clearest NT text supporting paid clergy.

The discipline procedure follows Jewish judicial practice (Deut 19:15). The solemn charge ‘before God, Christ Jesus, and the elect angels’ invokes a cosmic tribunal. The warning against hasty ordination (χεῖρας ταχέως μηδενὶ ἐπιτίθει) implies that some of the current problems trace to inadequate vetting of leaders—a lesson directly relevant to the false-teacher crisis.

The wine parenthesis (v. 23) is pastorally motivated, not theologically programmatic. It may push back against the ascetic abstinence of 4:3. The aphoristic closing (vv. 24–25) on the visibility of sins and good works supports the preceding counsel on careful evaluation of elder candidates.

towner.source: Philip H. Towner, 1-2 Timothy, Titus, NICNT (2006) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The elder section reveals a church in organizational development: leadership structures require not only qualification criteria (ch. 3) but also compensation systems, disciplinary procedures, and ordination protocols. The appeal to the OT for both remuneration (Deut 25:4) and judicial process (Deut 19:15) shows Paul grounding ecclesial governance in scriptural precedent. The invocation of the heavenly tribunal (v. 21) elevates elder discipline beyond mere administrative procedure to an act performed under divine scrutiny—a powerful deterrent against favoritism.

hist.context: This section addresses three distinct elder-related issues: remuneration for those who serve well (vv. 17–18), disciplinary procedures for accused elders (vv. 19–21), and caution in ordination (vv. 22–25). The two-witness rule for accusations (v. 19) draws directly on Deuteronomy 19:15, applying OT judicial procedure to church governance. The charge to impartiality (v. 21) ‘before God, Christ Jesus, and the elect angels’ invokes a heavenly tribunal as witness—a striking formulation that underscores the gravity of elder discipline.

st2.header: The Elect Angels: 1 Timothy 5:21 and Second Temple Angelology
st2.body:
1 Timothy 5:21 opens a solemn charge: 'In the presence of God and of Christ Jesus and of the elect angels I charge you to keep these rules without prejudging, doing nothing from partiality.' The phrase 'elect angels' (eklektoi angeloi) is unusual — it occurs nowhere else in the NT, and the specific adjective 'elect' applied to angelic beings has no direct parallel in the Hebrew Bible. To understand what Paul is doing, we have to look at the Second Temple Jewish angelology within which the phrase makes sense.

1 Enoch provides the most developed context. Across its five books 1 Enoch distinguishes between the 'holy ones' (the unfallen angels serving before God's throne) and the 'Watchers' (the angels who fell in the days before the Flood, whose binding and judgment 1 Enoch narrates at length). The 'holy ones' are implicitly elect — chosen to remain in God's service, contrasted with the fallen Watchers who chose rebellion. 1 Enoch 9:3 refers to 'the holy ones in heaven' who intercede for human suffering; 1 Enoch 39:1 speaks of 'the holy ones of the Most High.' 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 6, drawing on this tradition, imply the distinction between elect-and-faithful angels versus fallen-and-bound angels.

Paul's 'elect angels' invokes this same distinction. The adjective does not imply a cosmic election-by-faith analogous to human election (that would be theologically strange); rather it designates the angelic beings who remain in God's service as witnesses of the church's judicial-disciplinary proceedings. The charge in 1 Tim 5:21 has forensic-courtroom overtones: Timothy is charged before a three-fold tribunal — God, Christ, and the elect angels — to carry out ecclesial discipline with impartiality.

The forensic-angelic witness is also a Second Temple commonplace. Dead Sea Scrolls texts (1QH, 1QM) frequently situate judicial proceedings 'in the presence of the holy ones of heaven' as a witness-array. Qumran sectarian self-understanding included the conviction that the community's worship and discipline were conducted in the presence of the angelic host. Hebrews 12:22's 'innumerable angels in festal gathering' draws on the same imagery. Paul in 1 Tim 5:21 is invoking this shared Second Temple vocabulary: ecclesial discipline is conducted before witnesses that include the cosmic heavenly assembly.

The theological move is pastoral: the church's internal judicial life is not a parochial matter. Angels watch; the cosmos is invested; the community's discipline has stakes that reach beyond the congregation's walls. The Second Temple vocabulary serves a specifically Christian pastoral-ecclesiological purpose.

The canonical implications: 1 Enoch's angelology is the fullest extant source for the 'holy ones' vs. 'watchers' distinction Paul presupposes, but the tradition was broadly diffused by Paul's day. 1 Enoch's canonicity in the Ethiopian tradition and non-canonical status elsewhere does not change the fact that its angelology shaped 1st-century Jewish-Christian vocabulary. Paul can invoke 'elect angels' knowing his readers will understand.

citation_refs.source: 1 Enoch 9:3, 39:1 (holy ones of heaven); broader Second Temple elect-angels tradition

scholar_voices.note: Mounce treats 'elect angels' as a reference to the unfallen angels, distinguished from the fallen Watchers of 1 Enoch tradition. The phrase invokes Second Temple Jewish angelology as a courtroom-witness image for Timothy's disciplinary charge.

Towner notes the forensic-courtroom register of 1 Tim 5:21 — God, Christ, elect angels as a three-fold tribunal before whom Timothy is charged to act impartially. The tradition of angelic witness to ecclesial discipline is well-attested in Second Temple sectarian and apocalyptic literature.

Nickelsburg traces the 'holy ones of heaven' / 'elect angels' / 'watchers who fell' distinction across 1 Enoch's five books. By the 1st century this distinction was broadly diffused in Jewish apocalyptic; Paul presupposes it in 1 Tim 5:21.

st2.takeaway: 1 Tim 5:21's 'elect angels' invokes the Second Temple distinction between unfallen 'holy ones' (1 Enoch) and fallen Watchers. The three-fold tribunal (God + Christ + elect angels) frames ecclesial discipline as cosmically witnessed. Paul uses the Jewish apocalyptic vocabulary for a specifically Christian pastoral-ecclesiological purpose.

themes.note: Justice dominates in the elder discipline procedures and the equitable treatment of widows. Holiness pervades the character requirements and purity standards. Mercy and Judgment are held in tension throughout the welfare and disciplinary instructions.

rows.label: vv.3–8
rows.text: Care for genuine widows; family responsibility

rows.label: vv.9–16
rows.text: Enrolled widow order and younger widow counsel

lit.note: Chapter 5 is the most practically oriented section of the letter, addressing the social organization of the church as God’s household: family-style pastoral relationships (vv. 1–2), widow care and enrolled service (vv. 3–16), and elder governance (vv. 17–25).

hebtext.note: The noun χήρα (chēra) appears nine times in this chapter alone, more than in any other NT chapter. In the Greco-Roman world, widows were among the most vulnerable members of society, lacking the legal and economic protections that marriage provided. The early church developed a formal system of widow care (as early as Acts 6:1) that evolved into an enrolled order with specific qualifications (vv. 9–10). Paul’s instructions here represent the most detailed social-welfare legislation in the NT.

hebtext.gloss: to be enrolled, to be put on the list
hebtext.note: The verb καταλέγω (katalegō) appears only here in the NT and specifically denotes formal enrollment on an official register. This is not casual membership but a recognized ecclesial order with binding commitments. The enrolled widow pledged herself to prayer and service, receiving financial support in return—an early form of what would later develop into women’s religious orders.

The phrase διπλῆς τιμῆς (double honor) is debated: does τιμή mean ‘honor’ in the abstract sense or ‘honorarium/payment’? The context (v. 18, quoting Deut 25:4 and citing the worker’s wage) strongly supports a financial component: elders who lead well deserve both respect and remuneration. The ‘double’ likely means ‘generous’ rather than literally twice a specific amount.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

ppl.name: Christ Jesus
ppl.text: 21) ‘before God, Christ Jesus, and the elect angels’ invokes a heavenly tribunal as witness—a striking formulation that underscores the gravity of elder discipline…

rec.quote: "The phrase 'truly widows' (τὰς ὄντως χήρας) distinguishes between widows who have no family support and those who do—only the former qualify for church assistance."

James defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows in their distress, confirming that widow care was a cross-traditional priority in early Christianity.
OT legislation consistently protects widows, orphans, and aliens, establishing the covenantal basis for the church’s social responsibility.
Tabitha/Dorcas exemplifies the kind of widow ministry Paul describes: a woman devoted to good works and charity whose service was so valued that Peter was summoned to raise her from the dead.
Paul’s earlier teaching on the advantages of singleness for undistracted devotion to the Lord provides the theological backdrop for the enrolled widow order’s commitment.
Paul quotes this OT law (‘do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain’) as he does in 1 Cor 9:9, applying it to ministerial remuneration—a consistent hermeneutical move.

discourse.title: 1 Timothy 5 — Care for Widows; Honor for Elders
discourse.thesis: Pastoral instructions for relating to different generations and offices: widows in real need, families' first responsibility, elders worthy of double honor, accusations against elders, qualifications for restoration.

nodes.label: Different age groups (vv.1-2)
nodes.text: Do not rebuke older man harshly but encourage him as a father; younger men as brothers; older women as mothers; younger women as sisters in all purity.

nodes.label: Widow categories (vv.3-16)
nodes.text: Honor widows truly widows. If widow has children/grandchildren, they should learn to show godliness to their household first. The truly widow, left alone, sets her hope on God, continues in supplications and prayers. The self-indulgent widow is dead while she lives. Command these things. If anyone does not provide for relatives, especially household members, has denied the faith and is worse than unbeliever. Enroll widow if not less than 60, having been the wife of one husband, with reputation for good works. Refuse to enroll younger widows; let them marry, bear children, manage households. Some have already strayed after Satan. If any believing woman has relatives who are widows, let her care for them — let church not be burdened.

nodes.label: Elders + miscellaneous instructions (vv.17-25)
nodes.text: Elders who rule well are worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching — Scripture says, 'Do not muzzle ox while treading grain' and 'Laborer deserves wages.' Do not admit charge against elder except on evidence of two or three witnesses; rebuke persistent sinners in presence of all. Charge: do not be hasty in laying on of hands. No longer drink only water but use a little wine for stomach's sake. Some sins are conspicuous; others appear later. Same with good works.

discourse.note: The chapter's pastoral-practical detail (medical advice in v.23, financial-care arrangements for widows) preserves real first-century church-administrative concerns.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGive proper recognition to those widows who are really in need.KJVHonour widows that are widows indeed.NIVAnyone who does not provide for their relatives, and especially for their own household, has denied the faith and is worse than an unbeliever.KJVBut if any provide not for his own, and specially for those of his own house, he hath denied the faith, and is worse than an infidel.NIVThe elders who direct the affairs of the church well are worthy of double honor, especially those whose work is preaching and teaching.KJVLet the elders that rule well be counted worthy of double honour, especially they who labour in the word and doctrine.NIVStop drinking only water, and use a little wine because of your stomach and your frequent illnesses.KJVDrink no longer water, but use a little wine for thy stomach's sake and thine often infirmities.

coaching.tip: 'Treat older men as fathers, younger men as brothers, older women as mothers, younger women as sisters' (vv. 1-2). Church as extended family: relationships cross generations. The household of God isn't age-segregated but interwoven.

'Elders who direct well deserve double honor' (v. 17)—probably financial support, given the context. Labor in preaching and teaching merits compensation. The church that starves its leaders shouldn't expect vitality.

prayer_prompt: As Paul instructs Timothy on honouring widows and respecting elders, consider asking God whether the most vulnerable people in your community are receiving the care that His church owes them.
